preamble: The phonetic transformation from the diphthong /ui/ to /oi/ involves the lowering of the initial vowel sound. Specifically, the close back rounded vowel /u/ shifts to the mid back rounded vowel /o/, while the second element, the close front unrounded vowel /i/, remains stable. This change results in a more open articulation at the beginning of the diphthong.
phoneticEffects: Lowering of the first vocalic element of the diphthong

examples.note: The diphthong /ui/, particularly in words borrowed from Old French, evolved to /oi/ in some English dialects, a shift attested from Middle English into the Early Modern English period.
Doublets such as "onion" (from /ui/) and "oinon" (from /oi/), or "cullion" (from /ui/) and "cuillon" (from /oi/) illustrate this differentiation and subsequent shift.
In the pre-Haroi stage, the high diphthong *-ui underwent a lowering process to *-oi, specifically conditioned by a preceding voiceless obstruent.

sources: Sundén, K. F. (1904). The vocalism of Romanic words in Chaucer. Upsala.
Petyt, K. M. (2005). The Dialect of New Mills. White Rose eTheses Online.
Thurgood, G. (1999). Restructured Register in Haroi: Reconstructing its Historical Origins.
